pediatricBloodPressure.name: Pediatric Blood Pressure Calculator
pediatricBloodPressure.description: Blood pressure percentile for children and adolescents — AAP 2017 screening table.

meta.title: Pediatric Blood Pressure Calculator — AAP 2017 Percentiles | Health Calculators
meta.description: Classify blood pressure in children and adolescents (ages 1–17) using the AAP 2017 simplified screening table. Normal, elevated, stage 1 or stage 2 — free, instant, no sign-up.

pediatricBloodPressure.title: Pediatric Blood Pressure Calculator
pediatricBloodPressure.description: Classify your child's blood pressure using the AAP 2017 simplified screening table. Returns a risk category based on age, sex, and measured values.
pediatricBloodPressure.incompleteHint: Enter age, sex, and both systolic and diastolic blood pressure to see the category.

hints.normal: Both values are below the 90th percentile. Continue routine well-child checks per pediatric guidelines.
hints.elevated: Values are between the 90th and 95th percentiles. Repeat measurement in a few weeks and review lifestyle — most children normalise.
hints.stage1: Values are at or above the 95th percentile but below the 95th + 12 mmHg. Repeat at two further visits — if confirmed, refer to pediatric cardiology.
hints.stage2: Values exceed the 95th percentile + 12 mmHg. Seek medical evaluation within one week. With symptoms, attend the emergency department immediately.

pediatricBloodPressure.howItWorksText: The AAP 2017 guideline (Flynn et al., Pediatrics 2017) defines pediatric BP percentiles by age, sex, and height. This calculator uses the simplified screening table at the 5th height percentile — the most conservative, sensitive threshold. If your child's BP is below it, hypertension is very unlikely. From age 13 the AHA adult thresholds apply (120/80, 130/80, 140/90).
pediatricBloodPressure.disclaimer: This estimate is not a diagnosis. Accurate classification requires multiple measurements on different days, the correct cuff size, and clinical evaluation. Symptoms such as headache, vision changes, or chest pain warrant immediate medical attention.

faq.q: What ages does this calculator cover?
faq.a: Children and adolescents from 1 to 17 years. Infants under 1 year use different reference values — please consult your pediatrician. For adults, use our standard blood pressure calculator.

faq.q: Why do pediatric BP thresholds depend on age and sex?
faq.a: Blood pressure rises with growth and maturation. Boys and girls diverge especially after puberty. That is why pediatric BP percentiles are sex- and age-specific — a reading of 120/80 is pathologic for a 5-year-old but normal for a 16-year-old.
The cuff bladder must encircle 80 % of the arm circumference and cover ~40 % of arm length. A cuff that is too small falsely elevates the reading — one of the most common errors in pediatric BP.

faq.q: How often should children have their BP checked?
faq.a: Per AAP, BP should be measured at every well-child visit from age 3. Children with risk factors (obesity, prematurity, kidney disease, congenital heart disease) need earlier and more frequent measurement.

faq.q: When is high BP a pediatric emergency?
faq.a: Very high readings (well above the 95th + 12 mmHg) combined with symptoms — severe headache, visual changes, vomiting, chest pain, altered mental status — are emergencies and require 911 / pediatric ED. Without symptoms, prompt evaluation in 1 week is usually sufficient.

faq.q: What causes high BP in children?
faq.a: Younger children mostly have secondary hypertension (renal disease, coarctation of the aorta, endocrine causes). Adolescents more often have primary hypertension, frequently associated with obesity, low activity, and a positive family history.

Encourage 60 minutes of daily activity, a low-salt vegetable-rich diet, adequate sleep, manage overweight, and limit screen time. Confirmed hypertension warrants a pediatrician visit and possibly a pediatric hypertension clinic.
